The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy to check for metastatic deposits before starting the esophagectomy. The gastropathic ligament is then taken, and a liver retractor is prepared for placement.
The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy to check for metastatic deposits before placing the liver retractor and beginning the dissection of the gastropathic ligament.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to mobilize the hiatus, carefully dissecting and separating tissue around the esophageal hiatus.

Following the hiatal dissection, the surgeon begins mobilizing the stomach, carefully avoiding the gastro-epiploic artery. Using the harmonic scalpel, they grasp omental vessels to create an opening, allowing entry into the lesser sac, and proceed with dissection.

Following the hiatal dissection, the surgeon begins mobilizing the stomach, carefully avoiding the gastro-epiploic artery. The dissection starts by grasping omental vessels to create an opening into the lesser sac, proceeding upwards using the harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon continues the dissection, staying well away from the greater curvature of the stomach, carefully reassessing the proximity to the gastroepiploic artery to avoid injury.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the stomach, staying well away from the greater curvature to avoid injury to the gastroepiploic artery. Attention is given to the position of the splenic flexure to prevent an inadvertent colotomy.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the short gastric vessels, being mindful of the high-riding splenic flexure to avoid an inadvertent colotomy.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the short gastric vessels, moving closer to the greater curvature of the stomach as the gastroepiploic artery is no longer prominent.

The surgeon continues the dissection closer to the greater curvature of the stomach, as the gastroepiploic artery is no longer prominently visible, allowing for safer manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects towards the pylorus, maintaining visibility of the gastroepiploic artery to prevent significant bleeding from the short gastric vessels.
The surgeon maintains visibility of the gastroepiploic artery while continuing dissection towards the pylorus, carefully taking down retrogastric adhesions without injuring the gastroduodenal artery.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes retrogastric adhesions, ensuring not to injure the gastroduodenal artery. Once the adhesions are cleared, the anterior surface of the pancreas becomes clearly visible.
After taking down the adhesions, the anterior surface of the pancreas becomes clearly visible.
The surgeon continues dissecting the retrogastric adhesions, progressing towards the left gastric lymphovascular bundle, carefully separating the tissue to expose this anatomical structure.
The surgeon continues dissecting the retrogastric adhesions, progressing towards the left gastric lymphovascular bundle, carefully separating the adhesions from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon gains additional length on the gastric conduit by carefully dissecting and taking down omental vessels. After mobilizing most of the stomach, attention is turned to the left gastric artery.
The surgeon gains additional length on the gastric conduit by carefully taking down omental vessels. After mobilizing most of the stomach, attention is turned to the left gastric artery.
The surgeon clears the peritoneal covering from the vessels and mobilizes the posterior aspect of the right crus to access the left gastric artery.
The surgeon clears the peritoneal covering from the vessels and mobilizes the posterior aspect of the right crus to access the left gastric artery.
The surgeon positions the stapler at the base of the left gastric artery and vein, using a vascular load to staple these vessels off, ensuring secure closure.

The surgeon positions a stapler at the base of the left gastric artery and vein, using a vascular load to staple these vessels. After the first firing, the exposure is improved, and an additional stapler firing may be necessary to complete the process.

After the first stapler firing, the surgical field shows improved exposure, revealing the distal esophagus. The surgeon prepares for an additional stapler firing to complete the stapling of the left gastric bundle.
The surgeon has achieved excellent exposure of the distal esophagus, allowing for the completion of stapling of the left gastric bundle using a vascular stapler.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to finish dissecting adhesions at the level of the hiatus. Attention is then turned to the creation of the gastric conduit.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to finish dissecting adhesions at the level of the hiatus. Attention is then turned to creating the gastric conduit.

The surgeon uses a gray load stapler to create a four-centimeter wide gastric conduit, carefully managing the vascular fat along the lesser curve to prevent bleeding. The apex of the stomach is pulled into the left upper quadrant to prevent folding as the stapling continues, ensuring a long conduit.

The surgeon uses a gray load stapler to create a four-centimeter wide gastric conduit, carefully managing the vascular fat along the lesser curve to prevent bleeding. The apex of the stomach is pulled into the left upper quadrant to prevent folding as the stapling progresses, ensuring a long and stable conduit.
The surgeon uses the right lower quadrant port to pull the stomach down, straightening the gastric conduit to facilitate easier stapling. The next step involves placing a feeding tube in the patient.
The surgeon uses the right lower quadrant port to pull the stomach down, straightening the gastric conduit to facilitate easier stapling.

The surgeon identifies a spot in the left lower quadrant using a twenty-two gauge needle and places a stay suture from the abdominal wall to the anti-mesenteric side of the jejunum, approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz. Using an endo-stitch, the feeding tube is secured to the abdominal wall. The laparoscopic Barone feeding gastrostomy set is employed, with a dilator and feeding tube inserted, while sixty cc's of saline is pushed into the tube to facilitate distal advancement.
The surgeon identifies an area in the left lower quadrant using a 22-gauge needle and places a stay suture from the abdominal wall to the anti-mesenteric side of the jejunum.
The surgeon identifies a spot approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz and uses an endo-stitch to secure it to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic Barone feeding gastrostomy set to insert a feeding tube, accompanied by a dilator. The feeding tube is advanced while pushing approximately sixty cc's of saline through it.

The surgeon places an anti-volvulus stitch approximately three to four centimeters distal to the section of the feeding tube that is tacked to the abdominal wall, ensuring the tube travels as distal as possible.

The surgeon places an anti-volvulus stitch approximately three to four centimeters distal to the feeding tube tacked to the abdominal wall. The mediastinal dissection is then aggressively pursued, with the surgeon navigating unclear tissue planes due to the patient's prior neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy. The dissection is carried as high as possible to facilitate the subsequent VATS dissection.

The surgeon performs aggressive mediastinal dissection, navigating unclear tissue planes due to the patient's prior neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy. The dissection is carried out as high as possible to facilitate subsequent VATS dissection.
The surgeon enters the left pleural space, causing the diaphragm to appear floppy. The lung is visible, and the pleura is dissected if mediastinal fat is attached.

The surgeon enters the left pleural space, causing the diaphragm to appear floppy. The lung is visible, and the pleura is dissected if mediastinal fat is attached. The conduit is then sutured to the specimen before turning the patient to the left lateral decubitus position.

The surgeon sutures the esophageal conduit to the specimen in the anatomical position, ensuring the staple line faces upwards for visibility when pulled into the chest. The patient is then positioned in the left lateral decubitus position to begin the VATS portion of the esophagectomy procedure.

The surgeon begins the VATS portion of the esophagectomy by taking down the inferior pulmonary ligament, revealing previously exposed laparoscopic work. The ligament is mobilized up to the inferior pulmonary vein, with a diaphragm stitch placed for improved exposure.

The surgeon begins by taking down the inferior pulmonary ligament, revealing previously exposed laparoscopic work. The ligament is mobilized up to the inferior pulmonary vein, with a diaphragm stitch placed to enhance exposure.
The surgeon adjusts the diaphragm to prevent obstruction of the camera view, then focuses on the azygos vein, mobilizing it inferiorly and superiorly before transecting it using a vascular stapler.

The surgeon ensures the diaphragm does not obstruct the camera view, then focuses on the azygos vein. The vein is mobilized both inferiorly and superiorly before being transected using a vascular stapler.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the mediastinal pleura, extending the dissection down to the level of the azygos vein and further to the hiatus.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the mediastinal pleura, extending the dissection down to the level of the azygos vein and further to the hiatus.

The surgeon performs a mediastinal lymph node dissection and mobilizes the esophagus, working anteriorly to sweep subcranial nodes into the specimen side. The pleura is divided, and the aeroesophageals are transected using a harmonic scalpel, with clips applied to reduce bleeding and prevent chylothorax. The esophagus is swept off the pericardium, and once half is mobilized, the gastric conduit is pulled into the chest to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon divides the pleura and begins the mediastinal lymph node dissection, mobilizing the esophagus. They work anteriorly, sweeping subcranial nodes into the specimen side. The area is vascular, so the surgeon clips aeroesophageal vessels to prevent bleeding and reduce the risk of chylothorax. An en bloc lymphadenectomy is attempted, keeping lymph nodes with the specimen. The esophagus is swept off the pericardium, and once half is mobilized, the gastric conduit is pulled into the chest for easier esophageal dissection.

The surgeon adjusts the staple line of the gastric conduit to ensure it faces correctly as it is pulled into the chest. The conduit is disconnected from the esophagus, and the esophagus is dissected medially with liberal use of clips. The dissection continues at the level of the azygos vein, with improved exposure due to the upward traction on the esophagus.

The surgeon adjusts the staple line of the gastric conduit to ensure it faces the viewer, disconnects the conduit from the esophagus, and uses clips liberally during medial dissection of the esophagus. The dissection continues at the level of the azygos vein, with improved exposure due to the upward pull on the esophagus.
The surgeon identifies the thoracic duct, clips it, and then transects it. After mobilizing the esophagus to the maximum extent, the esophagus is transected using a blue load stapler.

The surgeon identifies and clips the thoracic duct before transecting it. The esophagus is mobilized to its maximum extent, and then transected using a blue load stapler inserted through the anterior ten millimeter port.

The surgeon inserts the stapler through the anterior ten millimeter port to transect the esophagus, followed by the use of an Orville stapler to create an anastomosis. The anvil is passed through the mouth using an orogastric tube, and a small hole is made on the esophageal staple line to pull the anvil through. The blue string is cut to detach the tube from the anvil, which is held with a Swedish DeBakey forceps. A purse string is placed around the anvil to ensure a snug fit, facilitating the creation of two complete donuts when firing the EEA stapler. The muscle retracts slightly, but the purse string effectively brings the esophageal wall together. A gastrotomy is then created along the staple line, ensuring the gastric conduit is correctly positioned.

The surgeon uses the Orville stapler to create an esophageal anastomosis, passing the anvil through the mouth with an orogastric tube. A small hole is made on the esophageal staple line, and the anvil is pulled through. The blue string is cut to detach the tube from the anvil, using a Swedish DeBakey for assistance. A purse string is placed around the anvil to ensure a snug fit, facilitating the creation of two complete donuts when firing the EEA stapler. The muscle retracts slightly, but the purse string effectively brings the esophageal wall together. A gastrotomy is then created along the staple line, ensuring the gastric conduit is correctly positioned.

The surgeon opens the stomach, preparing suction to manage potential spillage of gastric contents into the pleural space. The stapler is passed into the chest, and the gastric conduit is pulled into the stapler using the 'foot going into the sock' technique. The needle is deployed and mated with the anvil, assisted by the Swedish DeBakey holding the white part of the anvil. The stapler is removed, and the completeness of the donuts is examined. An NG tube is then passed distally into the gastric conduit.

The surgeon opens the stomach and prepares suction to manage potential spillage of gastric contents into the pleural space. The stapler is passed into the chest, and the gastric conduit is pulled into the stapler using the 'foot going into the sock' technique. The needle is deployed and mated with the anvil, requiring careful manipulation with a Swedish DeBakey to ensure proper alignment. The stapler is removed, and the completeness of the donuts is examined. An NG tube is then passed distally into the gastric conduit.

The surgeon uses a 30-degree camera to visualize the anastomosis. After placing the NG tube, the gastrotomy is closed with one or two firings of the blue load stapler. An omental flap, if created, is used to cover the anastomosis and is sutured to the prevertebral fascia.

The surgeon uses a thirty-degree camera to visualize the anastomosis. After placing the NG tube, the gastrotomy is closed with one or two firings of the blue load stapler. An omental flap, created earlier in the abdomen, is used to cover the anastomosis and is sutured to the prevertebral fascia.
